title: Egg on Clothing
timeEstimate: 15–40 minutes; cold-only protein, raw vs cooked-on fork
stainChemistry: Egg cooks. That's the whole trap. Egg white is concentrated protein that sets above ~60°C — even warm tap water will instantly cook a raw-egg stain into the fabric the same way it cooks in a pan, and once cooked, the stain is locked in. Egg yolk adds fat that sits next to the protein and needs a separate Dawn pass after the protein step. The fiber matters: cotton and washable synthetics tolerate the cool enzyme + Dawn protocol. Silk, wool, and other delicate fibers go to pro from minute one because enzymes that lift egg from cotton would damage protein-fiber clothing too. The recurring trap on any fiber: hot water at any step (even warm tap water) cooks raw egg into the fabric.
whyThisWorks: Cool water keeps the raw egg protein soluble and rinses out the bulk before it sets. An enzyme pretreat (cool water only — heat damages enzymes AND cooks egg) breaks down residual protein. Dawn pretreat then handles the yolk fat. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing. The honest cap: cooked-on egg or hot-washed egg is often permanent because the protein has bonded to the fiber the same way a cooked egg bonds to a hot pan. Pro is the right call for valuable garments where cooked-on egg is suspected.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): egg white is concentrated albumin protein; egg yolk adds lipid + lecithin. Albumin sets irreversibly above ~62°C. Pro uses cold liquid Protein Spotter (protease pretreat) plus cold NSD plus optional 3% peroxide on white cottons. For cooked-on egg, sodium hydrosulfite at controlled dilution may help residual color but the protein lock is mechanical and often plateaus. Silk / wool / delicate-fiber egg = pro from minute one — enzymes damage these fibers.
defaultAssumption: Raw egg drop on cotton / poly-blend / washable synthetic clothing (cooking spatter, breakfast spill, kid mishap), fresh and not yet hot-rinsed or hot-dried. Cooked-on egg or hot-dried = often permanent. Silk / wool / delicate clothing = pro from minute one.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Silk / wool / delicate fiber = pro from minute one. Washable cotton / poly-blend / synthetic = home protocol. Scrape thick egg off with a plastic spoon edge. Flat-edge work only — never rub. Don't pour water yet.
Damp white cloth with COOL water. Press, lift, rotate. Cool only — even warm tap water cooks raw egg into the fabric instantly.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Tide Plus per label, COOL water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dissolve enzyme pretreat in COOL water. Apply to spot, keep damp under a cloth, dwell 20–30 min. Hot water above bath temperature damages enzymes AND cooks the protein into the fiber. Cool only.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot. Soft toothbrush 5–10 min. Cool damp-cloth rinse.
Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks any residual protein and yolk fat permanently.

steps: 1. Fiber triage — silk / wool / delicate = pro from minute one.
4. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water, 20–30 min — BEFORE any heat.
5. Generous Dawn pretreat for yolk fat, cool, 5–10 min.
6. Cool machine wash + AIR-DRY. Inspect dry before any heat.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Tide Plus / liquid Protein Spotter)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat cooks egg protein into fabric permanently
warnings: NEVER hot water — even warm tap water cooks raw egg into fabric
Order matters — enzyme FIRST (protein), Dawn SECOND (fat)
Enzymes set above bath temperature — keep COOL
Cooked-on egg is often permanent — pro for valuable garments
Silk / wool / delicate fiber = pro from minute one (enzymes damage these)

homeSolutions.agent: Egg Cooks. That's the Whole Trap.
homeSolutions.instruction: Egg protein cooks fast under heat. Even warm tap water will cook a raw-egg stain into the fabric instantly — same chemistry as frying an egg in a pan. Two hard rules: NO hot water (even warm — cool only) and NO hot dryer until cleared. The order matters too: enzyme pretreat first for the protein, Dawn second for the yolk fat. And one fiber rule: silk / wool / delicate clothing goes to pro from minute one — enzymes that lift egg from cotton would damage these protein-fiber clothes.

If there's a thick bit of egg on the surface, scrape it off with a plastic spoon edge or dull butter knife. Flat-edge work only — never rub. Rubbing pushes egg into the fiber surface where it locks in.

Damp a white cloth with COOL water. Press, lift, rotate. Cool only — anything warmer than tap-cold cooks raw egg into the fiber. This is the most common failure mode: people grab warm or hot water 'to dissolve' the egg, and the egg cooks instead.

Dissolve an enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Tide Plus) in COOL water per the label. Apply to the spot, keep damp under a cloth, wait 20 to 30 minutes. The enzyme dismantles raw egg protein at cool temperature. Don't be tempted to use warm water 'to help' — heat above bath temperature damages the enzymes AND cooks the protein.
Yolk has fat that sits next to the protein. Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 5 to 10 minutes. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks residual protein and yolk fat. If the egg has already been cooked or hot-washed, accept that recovery is partial — pro is the right call for valuable garments.

steps: 1. Fiber check — silk / wool / delicate = pro from minute one.
2. NO hot water (even warm). NO hot dryer until cleared.
5. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water, 20–30 min — protein FIRST.
6. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 5–10 min — fat SECOND.
7. Cool machine wash + AIR-DRY. Inspect dry.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Tide Plus)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on silk / wool / delicate fiber clothing (pro from minute one). Stop if egg has been cooked or hot-washed (often permanent on most fibers). Stop on specialty / dry-clean-only fabric (pro). Stop after 2 home rounds without progress.
warnings: NEVER hot water — even warm tap water cooks raw egg
Order matters — enzyme FIRST, Dawn SECOND
Enzymes set above bath temperature — keep cool
Silk / wool / delicate fiber = pro from minute one (enzymes damage)

neverDo: Never warm or hot water on raw egg — cooks it into fabric instantly
Never skip the Dawn step — leaves yolk-fat shadow
Never use enzymes in hot water — destroys enzymes AND sets protein
Never use enzymes on silk / wool — digests these protein fibers
fiberSensitivities: Cotton / cotton blend: tolerates cool enzyme + Dawn
Polyester / synthetic blend: tolerates cool enzyme + Dawn (oleophilic — extended Dawn dwell on yolk)
Silk: pro from minute one — enzymes digest silk protein
Wool: pro from minute one — enzymes digest wool protein, hot felting risk
Cooked-on egg: mechanical lock often permanent

materialWarnings: Egg cooks at warm temperatures — cool water only
No hot water / no hot dryer — cooks egg into fabric permanently
Order matters — enzyme FIRST (protein), Dawn SECOND (fat)
Silk / wool / delicate fiber = pro from minute one (enzymes damage)

escalation.when: Silk / wool / delicate fiber from minute one. Egg has already been cooked or hot-washed (often permanent). Specialty / dry-clean-only fabric. 2 home rounds plateau. Yellow yolk-fat shadow remains after Dawn.
escalation.whatToTell: "Egg on [cotton / poly blend / synthetic / silk / wool / delicate] clothing, [raw / cooked / hot-washed], age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [cool enzyme pretreat + Dawn cool + cool wash + air-dry]. No hot, no dryer. [Residual film / yolk shadow / cooked-on suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with combination-stain (protein + oil) chemistry; specialty silk / wool cleaner if delicate fiber

contextQuestions: Raw egg, scrambled, or cooked-on (eggs benedict yolk drop)?
Whole egg, white only, or yolk only?
Cotton, poly blend, synthetic, silk, wool, or specialty fabric?
Fresh, hours old, or hot-washed already?
Has anyone tried warm or hot water?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Raw egg on washable fiber is tractable IF caught before any heat. Even warm tap water cooks it. Cooked-on egg is often permanent because the protein bonds to fiber the same way it bonds to a hot pan. Yolk fat is the second-stage cleanup — polyester is oleophilic so extended Dawn dwell needed on synthetic blends. Silk and wool go to pro from minute one because enzymes digest these protein fibers along with the egg.
